file_name: Wang et al. - 2020 - Deep learning-based, computer-aided classifier developed with dermoscopic images shows comparable pe.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a deep learning-based computer-aided classifier using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to classify dermoscopic images of skin tumors (basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevus, seborrheic keratosis) and psoriasis in a Chinese population. The CNN models were trained on datasets of 7192 and 3115 dermoscopic images and their diagnostic performance (sensitivity and specificity) was compared against 164 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'Original Article' and describes the development and evaluation of a deep learning-based computer-aided classifier using convolutional neural networks (CNNs). It presents original experimental results, including dataset construction (7192 and 3115 dermoscopic images), model training, and a comparative reader study with 164 dermatologists, which constitutes original research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Deep learning-based, computer-aided classifier developed with dermoscopic images shows comparable performance to 164 dermatologists in cutaneous disease diagnosis in the Chinese population
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated deep learning-based computer-aided classifiers using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to diagnose skin tumors and psoriasis in a Chinese population. The models were trained on two datasets of dermoscopic images collected from Peking Union Medical College Hospital between 2016 and 2018, consisting of 7,192 images for a multi-class tumor model and 3,115 images for a two-class psoriasis model. The diagnostic performance of these CNNs was compared against 164 dermatologists in a reader study using 130 dermoscopic images. The results showed that the CNNs achieved accuracies of 81.49% and 77.02% for the multi-class and two-class tasks, respectively, with sensitivities and specificities comparable to those of the dermatologists. Statistical analysis revealed no significant difference in Kappa coefficients between the AI models and the dermatologists (P > 0.05). The authors concluded that these CNN models could serve as effective pre-screening tools for skin tumors and psoriasis in primary care settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three main target diseases in Dataset I (multi-class model). All BCC cases were confirmed by histopathology. Included in both the multi-class model (368 images in development set) and the reader study test set.
One of the three main target diseases in Dataset I (multi-class model). Included in both the multi-class model (1872 images in development set) and the reader study test set.
One of the three main target diseases in Dataset I (multi-class model). Included in both the multi-class model (1887 images in development set) and the reader study test set.

In Dataset I, this group represented images not belonging to BCC, MN, or SK to increase screening specificity. It included acne vulgaris, dermatofibroma, and cutaneous haemangioma. Included in the multi-class model (3065 images in development set) and the reader study test set.
Target class in Dataset II (two-class model). Included in the two-class model (2101 images in development set) and the reader study test set.

Comparator class in Dataset II (two-class model) to classify psoriasis from other inflammatory diseases. Included in the two-class model (1014 images in development set) and the reader study test set.

Explicitly excluded from the classification list because the incidence in the Chinese population is relatively low and the number of pictures was limited. Mentioned in the discussion as not being part of the study's skin tumors.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin diseases (Basal cell carcinoma, Melanocytic nevus, Seborrheic keratosis, Others) and (Psoriasis vs. Other inflammatory diseases)
all_task_types.details: The paper developed two CNN-based classifiers: a multi-class model to differentiate three common skin tumors and other diseases (4 classes) and a two-class model to classify psoriasis from other inflammatory diseases (2 classes). The model outputs discrete class predictions, with performance evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and Kappa coefficients.

datasets_used.name: Dataset I (Multi-class Model)
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images of skin tumors and other diseases collected prospectively from the Department of Dermatology, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, between 2016 and 2018. All images were obtained using a MoleMax HD 1.0 dermoscope. Patients were Asian with Fitzpatrick skin type IV. Basal cell carcinomas were confirmed by histopathology.

use.training: Used to train the multi-class CNN model. The dataset was divided into training, validation, and testing sets in an 8:1:1 ratio.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (separated from training set to avoid overfitting).
use.testing: Used for testing the multi-class CNN model performance.
use.other: Images from the test set of this dataset were used in the reader study comparison with dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Dataset II (Two-class Model)
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images of psoriasis and other inflammatory diseases collected prospectively from the Department of Dermatology, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, between 2016 and 2018. All images were obtained using a MoleMax HD 1.0 dermoscope. Patients were Asian with Fitzpatrick skin type IV.
classes_used: Other inflammatory diseases (seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, lichen planus, pityriasis rosea)

use.training: Used to train the two-class CNN model. The dataset was divided into training, validation, and testing sets in an 8:1:1 ratio.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (separated from training set to avoid overfitting).
use.testing: Used for testing the two-class CNN model performance.
use.other: Images from the test set of this dataset were used in the reader study comparison with dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Reader Study Test Set
datasets_used.description: A subset of dermoscopic images from the testing sets of Dataset I and Dataset II, used for a comparative reader study involving 164 dermatologists. The images were collected in 2018.

use.testing: Used to evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, Kappa coefficient) of the CNN models against 164 dermatologists.
use.other: Served as the reference for the external comparison study.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing over 1.28 million images for over 1000 normal life objects, used for pre-training the CNN architecture.

use.training: Used as the source for pre-trained CNN parameters (GoogLeNet Inception v3) to initialize the model before retraining on the specific dermoscopic datasets.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To verify the robustness of the CNN models.
To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity and specificity metrics for both dermatologists and CNN models.
To assess the consistency/agreement between the classifiers (dermatologists and CNN) and the reference standard.
To assess statistical differences in Kappa coefficients between dermatologists and the CNN.
To visualize and evaluate the performance of the multiclass models by showing true vs. predicted labels.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the models for each category.
To determine the significance of the differences in Kappa coefficients.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the Kappa coefficients between the dermatologists and the CNN models.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the Kappa coefficients between the dermatologists and the CNN models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study found no significant difference in Kappa coefficients between the AI models and the dermatologists (P > 0.05), indicating comparable performance/agreement with the reference standard.

Dataset I (Multi-class Model).description: Multi-class classification of Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Melanocytic nevus (MN), Seborrheic keratosis (SK), and Others using 7192 images.
Dataset I (Multi-class Model).notes: No significant difference in Kappa coefficients between CNN and dermatologists (P > 0.05). CNN accuracy fluctuation was ± 0.88%.

Dataset II (Two-class Model).description: Two-class classification of Psoriasis vs. Other inflammatory diseases using 3115 images.
Dataset II (Two-class Model).notes: No significant difference in Kappa coefficients between CNN and dermatologists (P > 0.05). CNN achieved 0.797 ± 0.040 sensitivity and 0.719 ± 0.078 specificity for psoriasis in internal validation.

Reader Study Test Set.description: Comparative evaluation against 164 dermatologists using 130 dermoscopic images (70 for multi-class, 60 for two-class).
Reader Study Test Set.notes: Both dermatologists and CNN achieved at least moderate consistency with the reference standard. The gold standard was created by biopsy for BCC and expert consensus for all others.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on GoogLeNet Inception v3
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (over 1.28 million images for over 1000 normal life objects)

architecture_details.layers: 28 layers total (including input and output layers)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Feature maps obtained by applying linear filters and non-linear functions; hidden layer Ak calculated using tanh activation with weights Wk and bias bk
architecture_details.framework: Simplified framework shown in Figure 3; code based on TensorFlow Hub retraining example

training_details.data_split: 8:1:1 ratio for training, validation, and testing sets

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability for each disease category (summing to 1); highest probability regarded as classification category
tasks: Multi-class model (4 classes: Basal cell carcinoma, Melanocytic nevus, Seborrheic keratosis, Others)
Two-class model (2 classes: Psoriasis, Other inflammatory diseases)

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of images with poor focus
Exclusion of images containing multiple lesions
Exclusion of images with interference factors such as clothing, fiber, written notes, or hair (except for scalp psoriasis and scalp seborrheic dermatitis)
Exclusion of lesions located in the nail or mucosa
Exclusion of solar lentigo and lichen planus-like keratosis from the seborrheic keratosis category
Input vector format converted to numeric matrix (2592 x 1000 pixels)
Pre-training on ImageNet dataset parameters (GoogLeNet Inception v3)
conclusions: The performance of the deep learning-based computer-aided classifier (CNN) developed with a modest number of dermoscopic images for skin tumors and psoriasis in the Chinese population is comparable to that of 164 dermatologists.
There was no significant difference in Kappa coefficients between the CNN models and the dermatologists (P > 0.05), indicating comparable diagnostic consistency with the reference standard.
The multi-class CNN achieved an accuracy of 81.49% ± 0.88%, and the two-class CNN achieved an accuracy of 77.02% ± 1.81%.

For the multi-class task, the CNN demonstrated high sensitivity for basal cell carcinoma (0.800) and seborrheic keratosis (0.850), and high specificity for basal cell carcinoma (1.000) and seborrheic keratosis (0.940).

For the two-class task (psoriasis vs. other inflammatory diseases), the CNN achieved a sensitivity of 1.000 but a lower specificity of 0.605, whereas dermatologists achieved a sensitivity of 0.872 and specificity of 0.838.
Both the dermatologists and the CNN models achieved at least moderate consistency with the reference standard.
The developed CNN models could serve as effective pre-screening tools for patients suspected of having skin tumors or psoriasis in primary care settings.
The study demonstrates that a well-performed CNN classifier can be developed using a relatively small training dataset (7192 and 3115 images) provided the images are correctly labeled.
Malignant melanoma was excluded from the classification list due to its low incidence in the Chinese population and limited number of available images.
important_remarks: Population bias: The study was conducted exclusively on a Chinese population (Asian, Fitzpatrick skin type IV) from a single center (Peking Union Medical College Hospital), limiting generalizability to other ethnicities or skin types.

Dataset limitation: Malignant melanoma was explicitly excluded from the classification tasks due to its low incidence in the Chinese population and limited image availability, reducing the model's utility for melanoma screening.

Limited disease spectrum: The models covered only 11 diseases; the two-class psoriasis model included a limited set of comparator inflammatory diseases (seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, lichen planus, pityriasis rosea), and larger datasets are needed for broader differential diagnosis.

Input modality disparity: The CNN was trained and tested solely on dermoscopic images, whereas dermatologists had access to both dermoscopic and clinical images (including location, tactility, age, sex), creating an unfair comparison where the AI lacked contextual clinical data.

Small test set size: The reader study used a very small test set of only 130 images (22 psoriasis, 38 other inflammatory diseases), which may affect the statistical power and reliability of the performance estimates.

Potential class imbalance bias: The two-class model showed high sensitivity (1.000) but low specificity (0.605) for psoriasis, suggesting the model may be biased towards classifying images as psoriasis, potentially requiring weight adjustment during training.
Lack of external validation: The dataset was derived from a single hospital database, and the authors acknowledge that sensitivity and specificity might differ with diverse datasets.
No data leakage risk noted: Images were collected prospectively and divided into training/validation/testing sets with an 8:1:1 ratio, and ten-fold cross-validation was used to verify robustness.
